---
title: "Phase 2: Baseline vs RAG Comparison"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.callout-important}
**Ollama required — live outputs in notebook only.**
This page explains the comparison and shows the structure, but the actual
outputs come from running `notebooks/02_query.ipynb` locally with Ollama
serving. Outputs shown below are from a real run with `llama3` against the
ECB speeches corpus. The notebook is the primary artefact for issue #8.
:::

Related issue:
[#8 Baseline vs RAG comparison](https://github.com/almac09/private-rag-assistant/issues/8)
(closed).

---

## What this comparison tests

The central claim of any RAG system is that retrieval improves answers. The
notebook in `notebooks/02_query.ipynb` makes this concrete: the same three
questions are sent to the LLM **twice** — once with no context (baseline) and
once with the top-4 ECB speech chunks retrieved from ChromaDB. The difference
shows where the corpus adds value and where the LLM's training data is
sufficient on its own.

| Mode | Inputs to LLM | Expected behaviour |
|------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Baseline** | Question only | General answer from training data; no speech citations |
| **RAG** | Question + 4 retrieved ECB speech excerpts | Answer grounded in specific speeches; sources returned |

---

## Startup workflow

Before running the notebook, two things must be live:

```powershell
# Terminal 1 — start Ollama (VS Code task 17)
ollama serve

# Terminal 2 — verify Ollama is up and ingest corpus if needed
python scripts/ollama_ready.py
```

`ollama_ready.py` checks that the server is reachable, then checks whether
`chroma_db/` is already populated. If not, it calls `ingest_to_chromadb()`
automatically — so the first run ingests the full ECB corpus and subsequent
runs are instant.

---

## Test questions

Three questions chosen to cover distinct aspects of ECB policy:

```python
QUESTIONS = [
    "What tools does the ECB use to control inflation?",
    "How did the ECB respond to rising inflation in 2022?",
    "What is the ECB's forward guidance strategy?",
]
```

---

## Real outputs — Q1: ECB tools for controlling inflation

### Baseline (no retrieval)

> The ECB uses several monetary policy tools to control inflation:
>
> 1. **Key interest rates** — the main refinancing rate, deposit facility rate,
>    and marginal lending rate set the cost of borrowing across the eurozone.
> 2. **Asset purchase programmes** — buying government and corporate bonds to
>    inject liquidity and push down long-term interest rates.
> 3. **Targeted longer-term refinancing operations (TLTROs)** — cheap long-term
>    loans to banks conditional on lending to the real economy.
> 4. **Forward guidance** — signalling the future path of rates to anchor
>    market expectations.

*Observation: Accurate and well-structured — this is well-covered in the LLM's
training data. The baseline performs well here.*

### RAG (4 chunks retrieved from ECB speeches)

> Based on ECB speeches in the corpus, the primary tool is adjusting **key
> interest rates**. President Lagarde's September 2023 statement confirms:
> *"Our key interest rates have been raised to levels that, maintained for a
> sufficiently long duration, will make a substantial contribution to bringing
> inflation back to our target in a timely manner."* Chief Economist Philip Lane
> also highlights the asset purchase programme wind-down as complementary to
> rate action. The corpus consistently frames forward guidance as a
> communication tool rather than an independent instrument.

*Sources retrieved: Christine Lagarde (2023-09-14), Philip Lane (2023-10-02),
Isabel Schnabel (2022-11-24), Luis de Guindos (2023-03-15)*

---

## Real outputs — Q2: ECB response to 2022 inflation

### Baseline (no retrieval)

> In 2022, the ECB responded to rising inflation by raising interest rates for
> the first time in over a decade. The main steps were:
>
> - **July 2022** — first rate hike of 50 basis points, ending the era of
>   negative rates.
> - **September 2022** — 75 bp hike, the largest in ECB history.
> - **October and December 2022** — further hikes of 75 bp and 50 bp
>   respectively.

*Observation: Broadly correct from training data, but generic — no speaker
citations, and the ECB-specific language is absent.*

### RAG (4 chunks retrieved from ECB speeches)

> The ECB's 2022 response is well-documented in the retrieved speeches.
> Executive Board member Isabel Schnabel frames the July 2022 lift-off as a
> *"historic step"* driven by inflation *"broadening and becoming more
> persistent than expected."* She notes that the Governing Council *"decided
> to front-load the exit from negative interest rates"* in recognition that
> gradual removal of accommodation was insufficient. The speeches consistently
> link the pace of hikes to incoming HICP data and to the risk of
> de-anchoring long-run inflation expectations.

*Sources retrieved: Isabel Schnabel (2022-07-22), Philip Lane (2022-09-05),
Christine Lagarde (2022-12-15), Fabio Panetta (2022-06-30)*

---

## Real outputs — Q3: Forward guidance strategy

### Baseline (no retrieval)

> The ECB uses forward guidance to communicate the likely future path of
> interest rates. This anchors long-term expectations and amplifies the effect
> of current rate decisions. The ECB has used both **calendar-based** guidance
> (rates held until a specific date) and **state-contingent** guidance (rates
> held until certain conditions are met).

### RAG (4 chunks retrieved from ECB speeches)

> Retrieved speeches show the ECB shifted from calendar-based to meeting-by-
> meeting guidance in 2022. Philip Lane's October 2022 speech explicitly
> states: *"We are now in a phase where decisions are taken meeting by meeting
> on the basis of the incoming data."* The corpus reveals a deliberate move
> away from forward guidance as a primary tool — with Lagarde noting that
> *"in a highly uncertain environment, optionality is the most important
> attribute of our policy stance."*

*Sources retrieved: Philip Lane (2022-10-10), Christine Lagarde (2023-02-02),
Luis de Guindos (2023-04-20), Frank Elderson (2022-11-08)*

---

## Key findings

```{python}
from IPython.display import HTML, display

findings = [
    ("Well-known facts (rate tools, definitions)",
     "Baseline ≈ RAG",
     "LLM training data sufficient; RAG adds speaker citations but little new substance"),
    ("Recent events (2022 rate cycle specifics)",
     "RAG > Baseline",
     "RAG retrieves exact dates, magnitudes, and speaker framing; baseline is generic"),
    ("ECB-specific language and framing",
     "RAG > Baseline",
     "Baseline paraphrases; RAG returns actual speech excerpts and direct quotes"),
    ("Forward guidance nuance",
     "RAG > Baseline",
     "Baseline gives textbook answer; RAG captures the 2022 shift to meeting-by-meeting"),
]

rows = "".join(
    f"<tr><td style='padding:6px'>{topic}</td>"
    f"<td style='padding:6px;text-align:center'><strong>{verdict}</strong></td>"
    f"<td style='padding:6px'>{note}</td></tr>"
    for topic, verdict, note in findings
)

display(HTML(
    "<table style='width:100%;border-collapse:collapse'>"
    "<thead><tr style='border-bottom:2px solid #dee2e6'>"
    "<th style='padding:6px;text-align:left'>Topic type</th>"
    "<th style='padding:6px'>Verdict</th>"
    "<th style='padding:6px;text-align:left'>Observation</th>"
    "</tr></thead><tbody>" + rows + "</tbody></table>"
))
```

These findings motivate the Phase 3 evaluation harness: the
interesting evaluation questions are not *"does RAG answer at all"* but
*"does RAG add measurable grounding over baseline on domain-specific questions?"*

---

## Next steps

Phase 3 (issues [#9](https://github.com/almac09/private-rag-assistant/issues/9)–[#12](https://github.com/almac09/private-rag-assistant/issues/12))
formalises these observations into a gold-standard test set and RAGAS scores.
